costs: Opportunity cost: no active reduction in segregation intensity or SES-sorting logic within remaining places, since contraction is size-driven, not composition-driven
No mitigation for compounding teacher workforce aging/shortage pressures interacting with demographic decline (F-live-demography-09, F-live-implementation_planning-07)
Potential settlement-viability harm where rural secondary institutions close due to combined demographic and outflow pressures, an effect noted as under-audited (F-live-school_network_planning-10)
content.equity_impact: Neutral-to-negative on sorting logic: reduces the absolute scale of the selective system over time but does nothing to change who is selected or to address Roma segregation, primary-choice sorting, or the family-capital admissions advantage; equity gains, if any, are incidental rather than designed
expected_benefits: Requires no legislative capital, no confrontation with the directors' lobby, and no constitutional tension with free-choice guarantees, since no new rule is introduced
Provides a genuine, evidence-grounded counterfactual against which the added value of T1-T4 can be measured

Already partially observable in evidence: urban schools see 20-30% grade-7 class-size drops as students leave for gimnázium, while rural schools already show minimal selective-track uptake, indicating the passive dynamic is underway (F-live-demography-03)
content.goal: Serve as the explicit counterfactual: allow shrinking birth cohorts and statutory class-size minimums to force network consolidation and a natural decline in the selective track's absolute size and share, absent any new active equity or structural policy

implementation_steps.action: Continue current practice of applying statutory class-size minimums without new equity or capacity policy, allowing natural attrition of under-enrolled tracks
Process school-network adjustments (mergers, closures) reactively as local cohorts fall below viability thresholds, using existing administrative procedures
Track the passive decline in absolute and share terms of 6/8-year enrollment as an explicit monitoring baseline for comparison with active-policy scenarios

mechanisms: Structural, multi-decade fertility and cohort decline reducing absolute demand for all track types including 6/8-year gimnázium (F-live-demography-01, F-live-demography-02)

Statutory minimum class-size thresholds (26 recommended/28/34 maximum) becoming mechanically unenforceable in shrinking rural and low-density regions, forcing de facto closure or consolidation of viable intake classes independent of policy preference (F-live-demography-05)

Regional child-population contraction (10-30% in Western Transdanubia, Southern Baranya, Northern Hungary) creating structural viability challenges for multi-track pipelines even absent any equity-motivated reform (F-live-demography-04)

Existing geographic concentration of tracks in urban/Budapest areas means rural contraction disproportionately affects already-marginal rural provision, while urban flagship oversubscription may persist longer (F-live-school_network_planning-05, F-live-demography-03)
risks: Passive contraction is geographically uneven: urban flagship schools with path-dependent prestige may retain oversubscription and selectivity intensity even as rural provision withers, potentially worsening geographic equity even as national aggregate share falls (F-live-school_network_planning-05, F-live-demography-04)
Without any composition-focused rule, the SES/family-capital sorting mechanism identified across the evidence record continues unabated among whatever reduced capacity remains

Church/private schools outside demographic-driven state consolidation pressure (or with different capacity dynamics) could see relatively increased share, altering sector composition beyond public-sector planning intent (F-live-legal_and_governance-07, F-live-implementation_planning-05)
content.title: Demographically-driven passive contraction (no-new-policy baseline)
